codex_consult.feedback_summary: Blocked by tool-policy.v1: tool Skill not in allowed_tools for architect role. Self-review substituted.
codex_consult.feedback_incorporated: self-review substituted — 6 adversarial questions examined below

app_understanding.note: This is a pure code/configuration architecture review task. No running app to test. All analysis is from static code reading of commands, hooks, agents, and settings.

console_errors_checked.note: Not applicable — infrastructure/config review with no running app

network_requests_analyzed.note: Not applicable — infrastructure/config review with no running app

code_files_reviewed.note: commit.md, push.md, merge.md, pull.md, push.sh, merge.sh, pull.sh, pretool-git-privilege-guard.py, changelog-analyst.md, close.md, dev.md, settings.json, git-edge-case-analyst.md, pretool-workflow-gate.py

issues.title: disable-model-invocation: true blocks SlashCommand self-dispatch but does NOT block Agent tool calls inside the command body
issues.description: push.md, merge.md, and pull.md all carry disable-model-invocation: true in their frontmatter. CLAUDE.md rule 15 clarifies: this flag blocks SlashCommand-based self-invocation; Skill tool calls (and by extension Agent tool calls) are NOT blocked by this flag. However, the orchestrator-gate (pretool-orchestrator-gate.py) IS enforced on Agent tool calls. Adding agentic intelligence to push/merge/pull would require the command bodies to use the Agent tool — which is allowed from within a slash-command execution context. This means the architectural path (command body dispatches an analyst subagent via Agent, waits for result, then executes the wrapper) is technically feasible given the existing hook topology.
issues.measured: disable-model-invocation: true present in push.md, merge.md, pull.md
issues.expected: Understanding of what this flag does and does not block is required for correct design
issues.runtime_evidence: Static code reading of command frontmatter and CLAUDE.md rule 15
issues.observation_notes: disable-model-invocation: true prevents Skill(push:*), Skill(merge:*), Skill(pull:*) from being self-invoked (these are in the deny list in settings.json). It does NOT prevent the command body itself from dispatching Agent tool calls. The command body is the orchestrator context, and orchestrator-gate.py whitelists Agent.

issues.title: push.sh already implements a form of pre-push analysis (push-gate token validation) but the intelligence gap is different from what /commit addresses
issues.description: The push-gate token written by changelog-analyst validates commit_sha == HEAD (ensuring HEAD has not moved since the commit that authorized this push). This is a correctness check, not an intelligence check. The /commit analogue for /push is not a close-gate (there is no 'close-push.md' equivalent) but rather the absence of any subagent that reads what would be pushed and makes a semantic decision. Currently push.sh checks: token present, SHA matches, branch not detached, remote exists. It does NOT check: what commits are being pushed, whether those commits contain sensitive files, whether force-push would silently overwrite remote history, whether the branch diverged from remote since the token was written. These are the natural targets for push agentic intelligence.
issues.measured: push.sh token validation covers commit_sha and branch identity only; no semantic review of pushed commit content
issues.expected: A changelog-analyst equivalent for push would read git log remote..HEAD and assess commit content before authorizing
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of push.sh lines 84-125 (push-gate check), push.md, and commit.md changelog-analyst dispatch model
issues.observation_notes: The push-gate token was designed as a single-use SHA-binding token (changelog-analyst writes it, push.sh consumes it). A push-analyst subagent would need to run INSIDE the /push command BEFORE push.sh executes. It cannot modify the existing push-gate token mechanism without affecting changelog-analyst.md, which writes those tokens.

issues.title: Two-token architecture risk: push-gate (from /commit) would coexist with a hypothetical push-analyst gate — these must be sequenced, not replaced
issues.description: If a push-analyst subagent is added to /push, the command has two distinct authorization checkpoints: (1) the push-gate token at /tmp/agentic-commit/push/<repo-hash>/<branch>.json (written by changelog-analyst, validated by push.sh's built-in check), and (2) a hypothetical 'push-analysis OK' token or sentinel. If the design replaces the push-gate token with a new analyst-written token, it breaks changelog-analyst's contract (changelog-analyst writes push-gate, changelog-analyst.md documents this as its Phase 10 output). If the design adds a second token, push.sh would need to validate two tokens. The pretool-git-privilege-guard.py only validates push grants (from push.sh itself) and commit grants (from /commit) — it does not validate analyst-gate tokens. Adding a third token type to the guard is a material change to a security-critical file.
issues.measured: _evaluate_push() at line 568 validates: CLAUDE_PUSH_COMMAND_ACTIVE env, push-grant manifest (branch+expected_head+remote), no forbidden flags. No third-party analyst-gate check.
issues.expected: Any push analyst gate must be validated either in push.sh (before it writes the push-grant) or as a new pretool hook, NOT inside pretool-git-privilege-guard.py without careful coordination
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of pretool-git-privilege-guard.py _evaluate_push() (lines 568-592) and changelog-analyst.md Phase 10 (push-gate write)
issues.observation_notes: The cleanest architectural seam for a push-analyst gate is: /push command dispatches push-analyst subagent (Agent tool), subagent reads git log remote..HEAD and writes a pre-push-ok sentinel, /push command validates sentinel exists, THEN push.sh runs (which validates push-gate token from /commit). This keeps the two gates independent and does not modify the security-critical guard.

issues.title: merge.sh exports CLAUDE_MERGE_COMMAND_ACTIVE=1 in its own subprocess — a pre-merge analyst subagent dispatched from /merge command would run in agent context WITHOUT this env var, which is correct but must be documented
issues.description: The merge privilege guard check (_evaluate_merge at line 474) blocks git merge unless CLAUDE_MERGE_COMMAND_ACTIVE=1 is set in the env. This env var is set by merge.sh when it runs git merge in its subprocess. A merge-analyst subagent dispatched from /merge via Agent tool would NOT have this env var set (it runs in agent context, not inside merge.sh). The subagent's job is read-only analysis (git log, git diff, conflict prediction) — it should not run git merge at all. This means the analyst-then-wrapper pattern is architecturally safe for merge: analyst runs read-only in agent context (no env var needed), then /merge invokes merge.sh which sets the env var for the actual git merge. The risk is the analyst saying 'no conflicts' and then merge.sh finding conflicts because remote state changed between analysis and merge execution.
issues.measured: merge.sh line 54: export CLAUDE_MERGE_COMMAND_ACTIVE=1. This env var is subprocess-local to merge.sh; agent context never has it.
issues.expected: Analyst subagent for merge does only read-only git operations; only merge.sh subprocess sets CLAUDE_MERGE_COMMAND_ACTIVE=1 for the actual merge operation
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of merge.sh line 54, pretool-git-privilege-guard.py _evaluate_merge() lines 474-485
issues.observation_notes: Stale-analysis risk: between merge-analyst running (reading git log branch_name..HEAD, checking for conflicts) and merge.sh executing (checkout + git merge), the remote branch could receive new commits. A timestamp-bounded merge-analysis token (e.g., expires after 60 seconds) would mitigate this, similar in concept to commit grant expires_at.

issues.title: pull.sh is the weakest candidate for an analyst subagent because pull operates on remote state that cannot be read without network I/O, creating a TOCTOU window
issues.description: A pull-analyst subagent would need to run 'git fetch' to see remote state, then analyze what would be pulled (git log HEAD..origin/branch). Between the analyst's fetch+analysis and pull.sh's actual git pull --rebase, the remote can receive new commits. This TOCTOU (time of check, time of use) window is fundamentally unsolvable without holding a distributed lock on the remote — which is not available in git. The current pull.sh does not do any pre-pull analysis; it stashes, pulls, and reports conflicts. An analyst would add latency (one extra fetch before the actual pull) and could produce misleading guidance if remote changes arrive after the analysis fetch. For pull specifically, the agentic value would come from post-pull intelligence (reading what was integrated, summarizing incoming changes, checking for conflict hints) rather than pre-pull analysis.
issues.measured: pull.sh executes git pull --rebase without pre-fetch analysis. pull.sh line 120: git pull --rebase origin BRANCH
issues.expected: For pull, analyst pattern should be POST-execution (summarize what was integrated) rather than PRE-execution (predict what will arrive), to avoid TOCTOU unreliability
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of pull.sh lines 119-152 (pull execution and conflict detection)
issues.observation_notes: Additionally, pull.sh is blocked by pretool-git-privilege-guard.py's forbidden plumbing list — line 630: 'pull' is in forbidden_suffixes for _looks_like_git_forbidden_plumbing(). This means agents cannot run 'git pull' directly. pull.sh circumvents this by running in a subprocess. A post-pull analyst is safe (only reads git log, git diff) and would not trigger the guard.

issues.title: git pull is in pretool-git-privilege-guard.py's FORBIDDEN plumbing list — pull.sh's subprocess-level execution is the only authorized path, and any analyst must not trigger git pull directly
issues.description: pretool-git-privilege-guard.py line 630 includes 'pull' in the forbidden_suffixes list that drives _looks_like_git_forbidden_plumbing(). This means any agent (including a pull-analyst subagent) that attempts 'git pull' in a Bash call will be blocked with 'BLOCKED: agent direct git plumbing is forbidden (R6)'. The pull.sh wrapper circumvents this because it runs in its own subprocess (child of the agent's Bash call), which does not go through the agent's PreToolUse hook. A pull-analyst subagent must be designed to run only read-only git commands (git log, git diff, git status, git fetch) — not git pull, git rebase, or git stash (stash is in the allowed list for 'list/show/pop non-destructive forms' but git stash push might be blocked). This architectural constraint means the analyst cannot replicate pull.sh's full workflow; it can only observe.
issues.expected: Pull-analyst subagent must only use read-only git commands. pull.sh remains the sole execution path for git pull --rebase.
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of pretool-git-privilege-guard.py lines 619-638 (_looks_like_git_forbidden_plumbing function and forbidden_suffixes list)
issues.observation_notes: git rebase is also in the forbidden list. Since git pull --rebase internally runs git rebase, the guard's regex match on the command text would catch a naive 'git pull --rebase' invocation. The wrapper subprocess pattern is the only compliant path.

issues.title: No generalized 'pre-operation analyst' agent pattern exists — each operation has a sufficiently distinct risk surface that a single agent type would be overloaded
issues.description: The /commit -> changelog-analyst relationship is 1:1 with a clear division: /commit does gate-keeping and grant-writing, changelog-analyst does all git work. For push/merge/pull, the analogous division would need each command to have its own analyst agent. The risk surfaces differ materially: push risks content of commits going remote (history leak), merge risks conflict resolution and branch lineage, pull risks incoming change integration and stash safety. A single 'git-operation-analyst' agent could handle all three by routing based on operation type, but this introduces a fat-agent anti-pattern where a single agent makes heterogeneous decisions. The existing git-edge-case-analyst.md is read-only history analysis, not a pre-operation gate. Three separate analyst agents (push-analyst, merge-analyst, pull-analyst) each focused on their operation's specific risk surface is more consistent with the existing pattern (changelog-analyst is narrowly scoped to commit workflows).
issues.measured: changelog-analyst.md: 476 lines, narrowly scoped to commit-only workflow. git-edge-case-analyst.md: read-only post-hoc analysis, not a pre-operation gate.
issues.expected: Push/merge/pull agentic intelligence should follow the 1:1 agent-per-operation pattern, not a unified analyst
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of agents/changelog-analyst.md and agents/git-edge-case-analyst.md
issues.observation_notes: The close-gate pattern (close.md dispatches QA once, QA writes the close-report) is another 1:1 model. Consistency with existing patterns favors three separate analyst agents.

issues.title: settings.json Skill deny list blocks Skill(push:*), Skill(merge:*), Skill(pull:*) — correctly prevents model self-dispatch, but does NOT affect Agent tool calls which is the correct dispatch mechanism for new analyst subagents
issues.description: settings.json deny section includes Skill(push:*), Skill(merge:*), Skill(pull:*). This prevents the model from self-invoking these as skills. The new agentic intelligence for push/merge/pull should be dispatched via Agent tool (same as changelog-analyst is dispatched by /commit), not via Skill. Agent tool calls from the orchestrator context are governed by pretool-orchestrator-gate.py (which whitelists Agent). The new analyst agents would be registered in agents/ directory and dispatched by the command body via Agent tool. This architectural path is consistent with how changelog-analyst works and does not require any changes to the Skill deny list.
issues.measured: settings.json deny: Skill(push:*) at line 240, Skill(merge:*) at line 237, Skill(pull:*) at line 239
issues.expected: New analyst subagents should be dispatched via Agent tool from command bodies, not via Skill tool — Skill deny list does not affect Agent dispatch
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of settings.json lines 237-253 (deny list for Skill) and commit.md Step 6 (Agent tool dispatch of changelog-analyst)
issues.observation_notes: However, settings.json deny list for Skill does affect the GLOBAL disable-model-invocation: true mechanism in CLAUDE.md rule 15, which states: 'Add Skill(name:*) to settings.json deny for every human-only command'. The existing deny list is correct for preventing agent self-invocation of push/merge/pull as slash commands.

issues.title: merge.sh has no audit log — push.sh writes to ~/.claude/logs/git-privilege-grants.log on success, pull.sh has no log — inconsistent auditability across the three wrappers
issues.description: push.sh (line 321) appends a structured audit log entry to ~/.claude/logs/git-privilege-grants.log after every successful push: timestamp, sid, remote, branch, head, nonce, ppid. merge.sh writes no audit log. pull.sh writes no audit log. If agentic intelligence is added to these commands, the audit trail of analyst decisions (what the analyst saw, what it decided) would be critical for forensic analysis of post-operation problems. The absence of structured audit logging in merge.sh and pull.sh is an existing gap that becomes more significant when these commands acquire agentic decision-making.
issues.measured: merge.sh: no audit log write anywhere. pull.sh: no audit log write anywhere. push.sh: audit log at line 321 (success path only).
issues.expected: All three wrapper scripts should write structured audit log entries on success, and agentic analyst decisions should be captured in audit artifacts
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of merge.sh (entire file, no log writes found), pull.sh (entire file, no log writes found), push.sh lines 314-323
issues.observation_notes: The close-gate model generates a close-report-<task-id>.md as its decision artifact. A push-analyst, merge-analyst, or pull-analyst could similarly generate a pre-<operation>-analysis-<timestamp>.md or .json as its decision artifact, providing an audit trail even when the analyst says 'proceed'.

issues.title: close-gate analogue for push/merge: the 'analyst gate' should be a time-bounded file token, not a conversational memory check
issues.description: The close-gate (/commit requires close-report with CLOSE: YES, mtime < 24h) is a time-bounded file artifact check. This pattern works because the close-report is written by QA subagent and validated synchronously before changelog-analyst dispatch. For push/merge, an analogous analyst-gate token would need to be written by the analyst subagent and validated by the command before executing the wrapper. The critical architectural question is: what validates this token? For push, push.sh already checks the push-gate token (from /commit). Adding a second validation inside push.sh would require modifying push.sh — a wrapper that is part of the privilege guard mechanism. Alternatively, the validation could happen in the /push command body itself (before invoking push.sh): check analyst token exists and is < N minutes old, THEN call push.sh. This keeps push.sh unmodified and adds the gate at the command layer — exactly where close-gate lives in /commit.
issues.measured: push.md: no analyst gate check. commit.md Step 3: close-gate validation before changelog-analyst dispatch. commit.md Step 5: commit grant write before dispatch.
issues.expected: For push/merge/pull: analyst gate check should live in the command body (.md file) before wrapper invocation, preserving wrapper script integrity
issues.runtime_evidence: Static reading of commit.md Steps 3 and 5, push.md behavior summary
issues.observation_notes: For merge: the analyst gate token could be scoped to branch name + default branch + timestamp. For pull: as noted in arch-5, a post-pull analyst does not need a pre-gate token — it runs after pull.sh succeeds.

clean_areas: pretool-git-privilege-guard.py: comprehensive, defense-in-depth, inline-env injection blocked, single-use grant pattern is architecturally sound and does not need modification for new analyst agents
changelog-analyst.md: detailed and narrow scope provides a good template for new analyst agents
push.sh: push-gate token validation and push-grant write are correctly implemented and should not be modified by new analyst layer
close-gate pattern: file-based time-bounded token is a proven pattern directly applicable to push/merge analyst gates
Skill deny list: correctly prevents self-invocation of push/merge/pull as skills; Agent tool dispatch path is clean for new analyst agents
key_findings_summary: arch-1: disable-model-invocation: true does NOT block Agent tool calls — commands can dispatch analyst subagents via Agent
arch-2: Push intelligence gap is semantic (what commits would be pushed, any sensitive files) not structural
arch-3: Two-token problem is real but solvable by keeping gates at command-body layer, not inside wrappers
arch-4: Merge analyst runs read-only in agent context; merge.sh subprocess sets CLAUDE_MERGE_COMMAND_ACTIVE=1 separately — these do not conflict
arch-5: Pull analyst should be POST-execution (summarize what arrived), not PRE-execution, due to TOCTOU with remote state
arch-6: CRITICAL — git pull and git rebase are in the forbidden plumbing list; pull-analyst cannot run these in agent context
arch-7: One analyst agent per operation (push-analyst, merge-analyst, pull-analyst) is more consistent with existing pattern than a unified agent
arch-8: Agent tool dispatch is the correct mechanism; Skill deny list is orthogonal
arch-9: merge.sh and pull.sh lack audit logging — gap becomes critical when adding agentic decision-making
arch-10: Analyst gate tokens should live at command-body layer (like close-gate in commit.md), not inside wrapper scripts

architecture_proposals.title: Analyst-gate token pattern for push and merge (pre-execution)
architecture_proposals.current_state: push.md invokes push.sh directly after verifying push-gate token from /commit. merge.md invokes merge.sh directly after resolving the branch name. Neither command has a pre-operation analyst subagent or semantic decision gate.
architecture_proposals.proposed_state: push.md and merge.md each dispatch a narrowly-scoped analyst subagent (push-analyst, merge-analyst) via Agent tool BEFORE invoking the wrapper script. The analyst reads relevant git state (git log remote..HEAD for push; git diff branch..default for merge) and writes a time-bounded analyst-ok sentinel file at /tmp/claude-<op>-analysis-<sid>-<nonce>.json. The command body validates the sentinel (exists, age < 120s) before calling the wrapper. The wrapper scripts remain unmodified.
architecture_proposals.measurable_benefit: Adds semantic pre-push review (commit content, sensitive files, divergence) and pre-merge conflict prediction without modifying the security-critical push.sh, merge.sh, or pretool-git-privilege-guard.py. Analyst findings are captured as audit artifacts. Mirrors the close-gate pattern proven by /commit.
architecture_proposals.risk_if_ignored: push and merge remain blind to content-level risks (sensitive files in commits, unexpected merge conflicts from branch history) that a subagent can detect in seconds.
architecture_proposals.migration_strategy: Add analyst subagent dispatch as Step 0.5 in push.md and merge.md. Analyst gate token validation as Step 0.7. Wrapper invocation unchanged. Can be rolled back by removing Steps 0.5 and 0.7 without touching any hook or wrapper.

architecture_proposals.title: Post-execution pull-analyst for integration summary and conflict triage
architecture_proposals.current_state: pull.sh runs stash+rebase+restore and reports git log --oneline -5 on success. On failure, it lists conflicted files but provides no semantic guidance on why conflicts occurred or what was integrated.
architecture_proposals.proposed_state: pull.md dispatches a pull-analyst subagent AFTER pull.sh succeeds (exit 0). The analyst runs git log HEAD@{1}..HEAD (commits that just arrived), git diff HEAD@{1}..HEAD --stat (change summary), and compares with local changes that were stashed/restored. It produces a structured summary of what was integrated, flags any semantic conflicts (e.g., incoming changes to files the user has recently modified), and writes a pull-integration-summary to docs/dev/ for auditability. On pull.sh failure (rebase conflict), a conflict-analyst phase is also triggered.
architecture_proposals.measurable_benefit: Zero TOCTOU risk (runs after pull completes, not before). Adds semantic value without adding latency to the critical path. Consistent with the observation that git pull is in the forbidden plumbing list — analyst never calls git pull, only reads post-pull state.
architecture_proposals.risk_if_ignored: Pull remains a black box: users see 'N new commits' but no semantic guidance on what those commits contain or whether they introduce risk to in-progress work.
architecture_proposals.migration_strategy: pull.md captures pull.sh exit code; on exit 0, dispatches pull-analyst; on exit non-0, dispatches conflict-analyst. Both are Agent tool calls after pull.sh returns.

architecture_proposals.title: Unified audit log for all three wrapper operations (merge.sh, pull.sh gaps)
architecture_proposals.current_state: push.sh writes structured audit log to ~/.claude/logs/git-privilege-grants.log on success (timestamp, sid, remote, branch, head, nonce). merge.sh writes no audit log. pull.sh writes no audit log. When analyst subagents are added, their decisions also need audit trails.
architecture_proposals.proposed_state: All three wrapper scripts write structured audit log entries to ~/.claude/logs/git-operations.log (separate from git-privilege-grants.log to avoid mixing push-grant records with operation records). Each entry: ISO timestamp, operation type (push/merge/pull), branch, result (success/fail), analyst_ok_token path (if analyst gate was used). Analyst subagents write their analysis decisions to docs/dev/pre-<op>-analysis-<timestamp>.json.
architecture_proposals.measurable_benefit: Complete forensic trail for all git operations. Currently merge and pull leave no machine-readable record of execution. Analyst decisions become part of the permanent audit record, enabling post-incident analysis of why an operation was approved.
architecture_proposals.risk_if_ignored: merge.sh and pull.sh are black boxes in the audit record. Adding agentic decision-making to black-box operations creates unauditable authority.
architecture_proposals.migration_strategy: Append audit log lines at success paths in merge.sh and pull.sh, mirroring push.sh lines 314-323. Use a different log file name to avoid merge conflicts with the privilege-grant log.
